title: RSS 2.0, Atom 1.0, Namespaces, and Extension Policy · Language Lens: Feed Go Atom Feed Entry
summary: The parser assumes RSS only, ignores Atom namespaces, treats optional fields as required, loses xml:base and language, and guesses extension meaning. Predict the reader decision, feed or API contract, Go and PostgreSQL ownership, bounded failure, and observable evidence before reading the governing source. Planned artifact: Add and verify the feed go atom feed entry increment in standards-aware syndication vocabulary map. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case pract…
objectives: Map Atom feed and entry identifiers, authors, links, text constructs, content, categories, dates, xml:base, and xml:lang.
Map RSS channel and item required, optional, repeated, GUID, enclosure, category, source, date, and extension constructs.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Feed aggregation and Go Predict Feed Go Atom Feed Entry · Theory Feed Go Atom Feed Entry
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go team handles case 1273 during a PostgreSQL claim and failover drill. Build deterministic Go evidence to prove RSS and Atom format semantics, expanded names, variance, and extension restraint; browser Go must remain deterministic and free of network, database, process, and host effects; then interleave one schedule, lease, fencing, goroutine, cancellation, or shutdown path and prove ownership. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, sockets, XML libraries, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, race, load, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. checks.description: The evidence note explains the model, failure risk, and verification in the learner’s own words.
